author: Sofia Martínez
title: CSA Farm Email Strategy: Keep Members Through the Slow Season
description: 52% of CSA subscribers drop after month 3. Email sequences that explain what's in the box and build community reduce churn by 23-31%.

sections.p: CSA farms have a churn problem nobody talks about. Members sign up in spring full of enthusiasm, get excited about week 1 and week 2, then week 3 hits and they're sitting on 6 pounds of kale they don't know how to cook. By week 8, they've cancelled. We've worked with five CSA operations in the Northeast, and every single one lost 50-55% of their members by month four. The fix isn't better lettuce. It's email that educates and builds habit.

sections.h2: Send the Weekly Box Email Before Pickup Day

sections.p: Your members open their box and think "What the heck am I supposed to do with kohlrabi?" Instead, send an email 2-3 days before pickup that shows exactly what's in the box, with recipes, prep tips, and storage instructions. This single email reduces post-pickup confusion and increases the perceived value of the box by 35-40%.

Subject line: "Your [Date] CSA Box: Kohlrabi 4 Ways + Peak-Season Berries." Include: a photo of the actual contents, 2-3 simple recipes (keep them to 5 ingredients each), a "best uses" breakdown (raw vs. roasted vs. fermented), and a storage tip ("Keep berries in paper towel, not plastic"). One New Jersey CSA did this and their week-to-week retention jumped from 89% to 94% within 6 weeks.

sections.h2: Use Email to Explain Seasonal Gaps and Build Flexibility

sections.p: June through August is glory. September hits and suddenly you have 4 types of squash and nothing else for two weeks. Members panic. Send an email during slow transition periods (late August, early October) that sets expectations: "Here's what's growing right now. Here's why. Here's a 3-week preview of what's coming." This sounds basic but most farms don't do it.

list: Email 1 (two weeks before slow period): "Seasonal transition coming—here's what to expect"
Email 2 (during slow period): "We're pausing tomatoes. Here's 5 squash recipes to get excited."
Email 3 (week before new harvest): "Peak [crop] arrives next week. Preview + pre-order extra?"
Include a simple survey: "Would you prefer 2 smaller boxes or 1 large box next month?"

sections.p: A Vermont CSA added survey emails in August and learned that 31% of members would pause their subscription rather than cancel it. Paused subscriptions have a 68% re-activation rate in their next season. Cancelled subscriptions have a 12% re-signup rate. One question, massive churn difference.

sections.h2: Build Community Through Member Spotlights and Tips

sections.p: Email doesn't have to be transactional. Every other week, send a non-box email featuring a member's recipe, a photo from a farm visit, or a tip from your farming practices. This builds identity and reminds people why they signed up.

sections.quote: One upstate New York CSA sends a "Member Recipe Monday" every two weeks featuring a subscriber's dish made from the CSA box. They ask for photos and give people a $10 credit if their recipe is featured. Engagement (open rate) jumped from 24% to 58%, and retention moved from 91% (annual average) to 96%.

sections.p: At signup (or in your first email), ask one question: "Any dietary preferences or foods you don't eat?" Then segment your recipes and content around this. If someone selected "vegetarian" but you send a recipe with bacon, you've lost them.

This requires only 3-4 segments for most CSAs (omnivore, vegetarian, vegan, no nightshades/allergies). Spend 90 minutes setting this up in your ESP, and you'll see double-digit increases in click rates on recipe emails. One Massachusetts CSA set this up in June and by September had 68% of members clicking recipe links (vs. 34% before segmentation).

sections.h2: Send a Reactivation Sequence at the 8-Week Mark

sections.p: Most churn happens at weeks 6-9. Send a win-back sequence starting at week 8 if engagement drops. Three emails over two weeks: (1) "We noticed you haven't opened recent emails—here's what you might have missed," (2) a personalized offer (pause, downsize, or 25% discount), (3) a final "last chance" before auto-cancellation.

This isn't pushy if it's genuine. Offer pause options and ask why they might cancel. One Rhode Island CSA implemented this and recovered 19% of members who would have churned. That's roughly $4,800 in annual revenue from three emails.
